segments.text: The next Sutta, 5.15.144.
Once while dwelling near Saketa at three-toned grove,
the exalted one addressed the monks, saying,
"'Munks,' Lord, they replied, and the exalted one said,
"'Munks, good is it for a monk from time to time to abide,
conscious of the distasteful in one is not distasteful.
Conscious of one is not distasteful in the distasteful.
Conscious of the distasteful in both, what is not and what is distasteful.
Conscious of one is not distasteful in both, what is and what is not distasteful.
Munks, good is it for a monk from time to time,
rid of all that, both the distasteful and what is not,
to abide in equanimity, mindful and self-possessed.
Munks, pressing toward what good mark or what good aim,
should a monk abide conscious of the distasteful in what is not distasteful.
He thinks to himself,
"'May the passion of impassioning states surge not within me.'
It is verily pressing towards this good mark
that a monk abides conscious of the distasteful
in what is not distasteful.
And pressing toward what good mark,
should a monk abide conscious of what is not distasteful in the distasteful.
May the defilement of defiling states surge not within me.
It is verily pressing toward this good mark that a monk abides thus.
And pressing towards what good mark,
should a monk abide conscious of the distasteful in both what is not
and what is distasteful.
May the passion of impassioning states
and the defilement of defiling states surge not within me.
It is verily thus.
And pressing toward what good mark,
should a monk abide conscious of what is not distasteful
in both what is and what is not distasteful.
May the defilement of defiling states
and the passion of impassioning states surge not within me.
It is verily pressing toward this good mark
that a monk abides conscious of what is not distasteful
in both what is and what is not distasteful.
Monks, pressing toward what good mark,
should a monk abide in equanimity, mindful and self-possessed,
rid of both the distasteful and what is not distasteful.
May never anywhere in any place, in any way,
within me search the passion of impassioning states.
May never anywhere in any place, in any way,
search within me the defilement of defiling states.
May never anywhere in any place, in any way,
within me search the confusion of confounding states.
It is verily pressing toward this good mark that a man abides in equanimity,
mindful and self-possessed, rid of both the distasteful and what is not distasteful.
That's the end of the Sutta. This is one of the Suttas where it's a bit hard to
understand, especially you hear it the first time, get a bit confused what is distasteful,
what is not distasteful. So first I have to explain that what is not distasteful is what is
attractive. So what is attractive gives rise to impassioning states. That means to passion
because what is attractive like seeing somebody of the opposite sex gives rise to passion,
that is impassioning states. And then the opposite is what is distasteful. What is distasteful is
what is lotsam and what is lotsam gives rise to defiling states of aversion. You feel aversion,
you feel repulsion, it's something lotsam. So here the Buddha is saying that once in a while
a man should abide conscious of the distasteful that is the lotsam in what is attractive
when you see something attractive you learn to see the lotsamness in that for example in a body.
Why? So that passion may not overcome you. And the second one
being the defilement of defiling states sorry the second one is conscious of what is not
distasteful in the distasteful that means in the lotsam for example you see a dead body
for example sometimes you go to a funeral somebody's funeral you see the body lying there
it's distasteful that means it's lotsam so you have to train yourself to sometimes to be conscious
of what is not lotsam in the lotsam otherwise you feel aversion if you repulsion some people
they cannot stand looking at the cops and they dare not look at the cops. The third one
abide conscious of the distasteful in both what is not and what is distasteful
that means in the attractive and in the lotsam you try to see the lotsamness of both
and then the fourth one conscious of what is not distasteful in both what is and what is
not distasteful that means in both the attractive and the lotsam sites you try to see the attractiveness
of it or at least not the not the lotsamness of it and then the last one you learn to be
abide in equanimity mindful and self-possessed so that in future you're if you can abide
equanimous then all these different states of attractiveness and lotsamness
states of attraction and repulsion will not sway your heart so to learn that to practice that
we have to attain a very equanimous state a very mindful state and that is to be found in samadhi
when our mind is in samadhi it is very equanimous it is strong and when it is strong it is not easily
shaken if you continually train your mind to be abide in that state it learns to be very steady
like a rock so that it's not easily shaken so these are the different trainings that we practice
in fact in all these trainings samadhi is very very important because without samadhi and sometimes
we know that we should not be moved by certain states and yet we are moved for example person may
have a man may have a wife and children and he knows he has responsibility towards the wife
and children and yet sometimes he sees the secretary young and attractive and he knows
it's wrong to have a start a affair with her and yet he's so moved by the sights the sight of this
beautiful girl moved by the sound of this beautiful girl etc then he does something that he should
not do because his mind is not strong enough becomes a slave of the senses that's why in all
these samadhi is very important now the next uta is five point fifteen five hundred and forty five
pursuing five things month one is surely cast into hell as the reward what five one destroys life
takes what is not given lasts after evil passions lies and lives in that in doubt and lives in the
idleness of liquor and strong drink indulgence barely monks pursuing these five things one is
surely cast into hell as the reward as the end of the sutra this are the five precepts and if we
break the five precepts continually and in a serious degree then we will definitely go to hell
the first one if we always take life like some people always go hunting and fishing
and takes what is not given that means you steal or you cheat somebody and cause the family to suffer
and that can bring us to hell also the third one is to commit adultery the fourth one is to lie
to a great degree not just small lie not a white lie but a big lie and the fifth one which is quite
interesting if a person spends his life in drinking liquor and wasting his time and and not doing his
what he should do carrying out his duties was his family etc then he can even go to hell for that
and the next sutra is 5.15 by 146 monks seek you not a monk for a friend whose ways are five what five
he works at work or in activities busy himself in affairs is at enmity with eminent monks spends
his life taking the long way the way without an object and cannot from time to time insight
arouse gladdened and satisfy one with Dharma talk verily monks seek you not a monk for a friend
whose ways are these as the end of the sutra so these are the few things that is not good for the
monk one is he's too busy with work and different types of activities the second one he's very
busy with affairs different types of affairs that means in work and duties etc third one he's at
enmity with eminent monks always quarrels and with which well-known monks and then the fourth one
spends his life taking the long way the way without an object that means he wears the rope but he
does not have an object yeah some people they wear the rope to try to get out of samsara that's the
best motive in wearing the rope try to strive to practice to get to end suffering another duty
which is not too bad also it's like a person spends his life as a monk teaching the dhamma
dhamma dutta monk there are some who like to write books spend their life as a monk writing books etc
so at least they have an object of course the best object is and the most noble object is to end
suffering but there are other objects but there are some monks who have no object and then the last
one this monk cannot arouse gladdened and satisfy one with dhamma talk so this type of monk the
Buddha says a monk should not get for a friend if the next car is 5.15.147 monks these five are
not a good man's gifts but five he gives without deference gives without thinking gives not with
his own hand gives what is rejected or thrown away he gives holding views as to the future
rarely monks these are the five see end of the sutta this describes how in giving these
these are the wrong ways to give the first one is to give without deference that means without
respect some people they they give like to a beggar they they give 10 cents or 20 cents they throw
it at the beggar that's not good because if you give without respect then there is there is
there is a vipakala fruit of it and a result of that but that's in the next sutta so i won't
explain here the second one is give without thinking that means mindlessly without paying attention
the mind is somewhere else when you give the third one does not give with his own hand that means it
is best if you give yourself but it's not always practical sometimes you're not able to go and
give yourself then you ask somebody to give it on your behalf it's also quite all right
because if if you can't go yourself to give then the fourth one he gives what is rejected or thrown
away that's not good at least if what is extra is still in good condition that's all right but
some people like i've gone on arms around once when i was living in ipo in a cave i went to this
new village for arms around and those people had never seen a mango on arms around so some of them
started to give one day i got a piece of bread with mold on it or did it found this on it so
it's not good to give what you want to throw away and then the last one to give holding views as to
the future that means some people give with expectations they think some people they think
when i give i should make a wish but it's not very good because it's a selfish thought
you're giving like trading you're giving in hopes of getting something in return and that's
that's not so good because there is a little bit of selfishness in it so the blessing the
merit is not so great the next futah is 5.15.148 monks these five are good men's gifts what five
he gives a gift in faith with deference in time with unconstrained heart he gives a gift without
hurt to self or others and in giving a gift in faith where so ever the full result of that gift
ripens there comes wealth riches and great property and he is fair to look upon handsome
but the wondrous beauty of the lotus and in giving a gift with deference where so ever the full
result of that gift ripens his children and wife his slaves messengers and workforce harken diligently
unto him lend ear and serve him with understanding heart and in giving a gift in time where so ever
that ripens there comes wealth and the goods that come to him in time are abundant and in giving a
gift with unconstrained heart where so ever that ripens there comes wealth and he bends the mind
to enjoy the fullness of the five fours joy of the senses and in giving a gift without hurt to
self or others where so ever the full result of that gift ripens there comes wealth riches and
great property and never from anywhere comes hurt to his property either from fire or water
rajas or thieves or impious heirs verily monks these are the five gifts of a good man that's the
end of the sutta now this is quite a good sutta tells you the result of giving with different
conditions a person who gives with faith as having a lot of faith because in giving you always
the result of giving is always that wealth comes back to you riches and great property
of course depending on how much you give and also depends on how virtuous you are and the person
to whom you give but when a person gives in faith having a lot of faith in addition to wealth
he is born very beautiful or very handsome and the second one when a person gives with respect
then the result of it in addition to wealth then the children and wife slaves messengers and
workers all listen to him and serve him with an understanding heart that's the result of showing
respect so because you show respect when you give then in addition to wealth those people around you
all show respect to you the third one a person gives in good time in addition to wealth he
he the wealth comes to him also in a good time when he needs it whenever he needs it
and giving in good time means when a person really needs it at that time and that is the time to give
like when somebody asks you for help and that person is really desperate and you pretend you
have something else to do or you have you have nothing to give him and another day when that person
is rich and then you go and maybe make him a gift but that's not the time he wants when he's already
rich you make a gift to him he doesn't appreciate it is only when he is very poor and he's in need
you help him that's the type of person I usually hear when we think about friends when we think of
those who have done us a service you all we always remember the person who helped us when we were
down and out when we really needed help that is the type of person who is a true friend
so when a person gives in good time like this he will also the wealth will come to him in good
time also then the another one the fourth one giving with unconstrained heart that means
there are some people give it a constrained heart you know in other words for example they
they want to give then they think I give ten dollars is too much I think I give five dollars
five dollars too much I think I make a gift of two dollars or one dollar so or sometimes he
make a gift after giving then he goes home then he regrets so if you regret then your blessing
is reduced so the result of it is next time when you be in your next life or something you you're
born and there's a lot of wealth you come by a lot of wealth you won't know how to enjoy it the joy
cannot arise because at that moment when you gave that gift you had no joy in you so next time
when the result ripens and great wealth comes to you you have no joy in it you don't know how to
enjoy in it there's a well-known story the dhammapada commentaries about a man who made a gift to a
pacheka buddha he was a very stingy person in that story and his wife liked to give to monks and
renunciants who came by the house and beggars and all that but he never approved of his wife giving
to these people who came to beg for food he thought he's wasting his wealth he thought he should
save all that food all that expenditure and instead i give it to his workers because his
workers will work for him in return so one day he was in a very good mood and he told his wife that
the wife could give whatever food she would she'd like to give that day so happened a pacheka buddha
came by and the wife cooked some good food and gave it to that pacheka buddha and when the pacheka
buddha left the house and walked away he happened this this stingy man happened to come back and he
saw this pacheka buddha this this monk going away and having come from his house he knew that this
monk must have got some food from his wife so he stopped the monk and he asked the monk to open
his or he opened the monk's bowl himself to see what was in the bowl and when he saw the good food
in the bowl he closed the lid and then he went back and i was not happy i thought his wife wasted all
his good food so because of that in the the the next seven times because of having given to a
pacheka buddha the next seven times he was born as the richest man in that town in that town for
seven lifetimes he was the richest man but every time he was born as a rich man he was so stingy
he used all the old things and he ate also stale food you know never knew how to enjoy his wealth
was as good as a poor man for seven lifetimes at the end of seven lifetimes having used up his
good fortune and heaven not having planted new blessings after that lifetime he went to hell
because his good karma bank account was depleted every time we come and enjoy life we are using up
our good karma bank account our blessings bank account if we don't know how to replenish it
all gets used up so we must know we understand the law of karma we know as we enjoy now we keep
saving doing good deeds and so that our blessings bank account our merit bank account
will increase and we can continue to enjoy life and then the last one giving a gift without hurt to
to self or others this is very important that means when you give a gift you must not harm other
beings you must not harm yourself or harm other beings means you must not go for example go to
the market and you select a chicken a live chicken or a fish and ask them to slaughter
and then after that you go and make an offering that is causing harm to others
causing harm to yourself means that you make that offering and it harms you in some way
and from this you find a contradiction with our jataka tales because in our jataka tales
there's a story about how the bodhisatta or bodhisattva in his previous life through himself
to feed down the hill committed suicide to feed to feed a tiger a hungry tiger and the cups
and this contradicts the sutta that's why that jataka tale is not quite acceptable because it's
not in the original discourses of the Buddha is there something that was compiled later
it's a kind of folk tale and also the other jataka story you know about how the Buddha gave away
his wife and children to a beggar that is also not acceptable because when you give away at least
if he had given away his wife and his children to a millionaire a wealthy man so that they have a
good life that is understandable that to give the wife and children away to a beggar who beat the
children and probably beat the wife and that is it's not logical so there are a lot of these kinds
of contradictions maybe when we study the sutta we have to think reflect on it then you will see
some of these points the next sutta is 5.15.149 the Buddha said monks these five things lead to the
falling away of a monk who is partially released what five delight in work in gossip in sleep
in company and he does not look at the mind as released early monks these are the five things
that lead to the falling away of a monk who is partially released as the end of the sutta what
is a monk who is partially released this would probably refer to an aria an aria is partially
released from bondage from samsara so these are the things that that obstruct a monk from progressing
further too much work and activity too much activity or work and then gossip talking with
other monks too much sleep and the fourth one is he likes to mix with others in company does not
he's not aloof there's a type of monk who is aloof there's a type of monk who likes company
and then the last one he does not look as the he does not look at the mind as released this is as I
mentioned before this is quite an interesting one that means basically our mind is not in bondage
it's only the thinking that makes us in bondage if I allow thinking to stop there is no
no suffering as we see it so these are the things that obstruct a monk and this is already mentioned
in one of the suttas earlier 5.16.155 monks these five things lead to the confounding the
disappearance of true dhamma satt dhamma part five here in monks the monks master not dhamma
this monk is the first thing that leads to the confounding the disappearance of satt dhamma
2. they teach not others dhamma in detail as heard as learned this monks is the second thing
3. they make not others speak it in detail as heard as learned this monks is the third thing
they make no repetition of it in detail as heard as learned this monks is the fourth thing
again monks the monks do not in their hearts turn over and ponder upon dhamma
they review it not in their minds this monks is the fifth thing that leads to the confounding
the disappearance of satt dhamma monks these are the five things that lead to the confounding
the disappearance of satt dhamma so these are the conditions that cause the disappearance of
the true dhamma earlier i think you remember i mentioned another sutta also in the chapter of
the force that conditions that cause the disappearance of the true dhamma one is that the monks are
stubborn they don't don't want to train in the training and other conditions that sutta was
angutra nikaya 4.160 this is another sutta there's another one also in the samyuta nikaya 16.13
so this one this sutta the first condition is that the monks do not master dhamma they don't
take the trouble to study the suttas because in the angutra nikaya 4.180 the Buddha said dhamma
refers to the suttas the original discourses of the Buddha and the four nikayas so if a monk
does not master the suttas he he will find it hard to differentiate between the true dhamma
and the false dhamma because there's such a thing as false dhamma mentioned in the samyuta nikaya 16.13
so the first thing is important for a monk to master the suttas the second one they do not
teach others the suttas the dhamma in detail that's the second one they do not teach the suttas
the third one they do not make others speak it in detail that means they're disciples because in
the days of the Buddha they didn't have books so to prolong the life of the dhamma they had to
recite it and carry it on from generation to generation so that they had to train the younger
monks to speak it to recite it to memorize it so this is the third condition if they do not
make others speak it in detail that means memorize it the fourth one they make no repetition of it
in detail as heard as learned this one is the monks themselves the senior monks they do not
repeat it this is by repeating it that they memorize it and also they understand it better
if you remember this one of the ways that a person attains aryahood then the fifth one
do not they do not reflect and ponder over the dhamma that they have learned
this is the fifth thing that leads to the disappearance because by reflecting and pondering
over dhamma we come to understand it better so all these are another condition for the attaining
of aryahood if you remember one of the earliest suttas mentioned that the five ways of attaining
aryahood is one in is learning the dhamma the second one is teaching the dhamma third one is
repeating the dhamma fourth one is reflecting upon dhamma the fifth one is during meditation
these are the five times first in attaining aryahood so here here this is these are the conditions
if you want to prolong the dhamma the first one is applicable to us now now we should master the
dhamma master the suttas because if you take no interest in the suttas it will cause the suttas
to disappear that was already worn by the buddha and then we should teach the dhamma also you know
as we know the dhamma so we should teach it to others the third one is not quite applicable
now to make others speak it in detail because we have it all written down in books the fourth one
repeating it this if we have the interest and the inclination is good to repeat the suttas as we
know them the fifth one is to ponder over the suttas this one is is quite important whatever
suttas whatever dhamma we hear we should reflect on it and try to understand it better
